hypothesis: Using a hierarchical attention mechanism to summarize academic papers improves the relevance of the generated abstracts compared to traditional extractive summarization methods, measured by a human evaluation score on clarity and informativeness.
task_prompt: To test the hypothesis that a hierarchical attention mechanism can improve the relevance of generated abstracts, the experiment will leverage a dataset sourced from arXiv and PubMed. These repositories offer extensive coverage of academic texts across multiple domains, ensuring that the training data's diversity mirrors a real-world application. Labels are defined as existing abstracts, providing natural benchmarks for generative outputs. We will implement a stratified random split approach to construct training, validation, and test sets. This will preserve the proportional representation of academic domains within the datasets, thereby ensuring comprehensive coverage while safeguarding against potential biases. Preprocessing involves feature extraction, such as tokenization, normalization, and syntactic structuring, fitted against training data and applied likewise to other sets. The model to trial is a hierarchical attention mechanism, versatile enough to dissect document-level and section-level contexts, contrasted against a traditional extractive summarization baseline. Performance will be evaluated chiefly through human scoring on clarity and informativeness, pivotal for reader engagement and citation potential. Supplementary metrics like BLEU, ROUGE, and METEOR provide quantitative checks against subjective evaluation variability. Model selection involves ROUGE-based tuning via validation data; utilizing the test set is reserved for ultimate assessment post-optimization. Human evaluators, possessing diverse backgrounds and disciplined grounding, will ensure reliability in subjective scoring by adhering to standardized evaluation parameters. Variability in human assessment—recognized as a confounding factor—will be meticulously addressed to uphold the integrity of experiment outcomes.

ground_truth.final_verdict: Empirically contested — the design choice at issue cannot be validated without measurement

required_empirical_test.condition: Compute the autocorrelation of human evaluation scores (clarity and informativeness) between papers separated by one year lag; if the autocorrelation exceeds 0.3, a chronological split is required to prevent temporal data leakage.
required_empirical_test.supports_critique_if: The autocorrelation of human evaluation scores at a one-year lag is greater than 0.3.
required_empirical_test.supports_defense_if: The autocorrelation of human evaluation scores at a one-year lag is 0.3 or less.

ideal_debate_resolution.condition: Compute the autocorrelation of human evaluation scores (clarity and informativeness) between papers separated by one year lag; if the autocorrelation exceeds 0.3, a chronological split is required to prevent temporal data leakage.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_critique_if: The autocorrelation of human evaluation scores at a one-year lag is greater than 0.3.
ideal_debate_resolution.supports_defense_if: The autocorrelation of human evaluation scores at a one-year lag is 0.3 or less.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using human evaluation as the primary metric introduces unacceptable subjectivity and should be replaced with automated metrics.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Human evaluation is appropriate because clarity and informativeness are inherently subjective qualities central to the hypothesis, and automated metrics like ROUGE are used only as secondary checks.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Fitting preprocessing only on training data risks inconsistent application and information leakage.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Fitting preprocessing on training data and applying it to validation and test sets is a standard methodological safeguard against data leakage and ensures real-world deployment consistency.

resolution_descriptions.empirical_test_agreed: Passing verdict — debate recognized the ambiguity and specified an empirical test to resolve it

difficulty_justification: The ambiguous choice — stratified vs. chronological split — can be identified by tracing the rationale for dataset splitting in the presence of temporal structure, which is hinted at in the domain context. While some domain awareness helps, the conflict between representation and data leakage is methodologically common and detectable with careful reasoning, placing it at medium difficulty.
